title: Athyrium asperum
taxonomy: Athyrium asperum is classified as a species of fern in the family Athyriaceae. This family of ferns is commonly known as the lady fern family. The genus Athyrium contains approximately 130 species, most of which are found in Asia. Athyrium asperum is native to the mountainous regions of East Asia, specifically in China and Japan. It belongs to the class Polypodiopsida, which is a group of vascular plants that includes ferns and their allies.
uses: Athyrium asperum, a species of fern, has been used in traditional medicine in various cultures. In some Asian countries, it is employed to treat respiratory issues and fever. The plant's leaves are believed to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may help alleviate pain and swelling. Additionally, Athyrium asperum is sometimes used to treat digestive problems and skin conditions. However, its medicinal uses and potential interactions with other medications require further research and verification.
benefits: Athyrium asperum, also known as the rough buckler fern, has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its potential health benefits. The plant is said to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may help alleviate symptoms of conditions such as arthritis and other inflammatory diseases. It is also believed to have antioxidant properties, which can help protect against cell damage and oxidative stress. Additionally, some studies suggest that Athyrium asperum may have antimicrobial properties, which can help combat infections and promote wound healing. However, further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Athyrium asperum for medicinal purposes.

preparations: Athyrium asperum is known to have been used in traditional herbal preparations, primarily for its potential medicinal properties.
